structured.summary: The paper introduces Later Span Adaptation (LaSA), a method to incorporate span-level information into pre-trained language models during fine-tuning, enhancing their adaptability and performance on various natural language understanding tasks.

strengths.point: The paper proposes a novel approach to incorporate span-level information during fine-tuning rather than pre-training, which is more resource-efficient.
The method shows significant performance improvements on the GLUE benchmark, demonstrating its effectiveness.
The approach is more flexible and adaptive to different downstream tasks compared to previous methods.

weaknesses.point: The paper lacks a detailed comparison with other fine-tuning methods that incorporate span-level information.
The segmentation method relies on a pre-sampled n-gram dictionary, which may not be optimal for all tasks or languages.
The paper does not provide a comprehensive analysis of the computational efficiency of the proposed method compared to existing methods.
There is limited discussion on the potential limitations or failure cases of the proposed method.
The hierarchical CNN architecture used for recombining representations is not thoroughly justified or compared with other possible architectures.

questions.question: How does the performance of LaSA compare with other fine-tuning methods that incorporate span-level information?
questions.motivation: Understanding the relative effectiveness of the proposed method compared to similar approaches is crucial for evaluating its contribution.

questions.question: What are the computational requirements of LaSA compared to existing pre-training methods that incorporate span-level information?
questions.motivation: Assessing the computational efficiency is important for understanding the practical applicability of the method.

questions.question: How does the choice of the n-gram dictionary affect the performance across different languages and tasks?
questions.motivation: The adaptability of the method to various languages and tasks is important for its generalizability.

critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks a detailed comparison with other fine-tuning methods that incorporate span-level information. (Related Work and Experiments sections)
critique_points.point_002: The segmentation method relies on a pre-sampled n-gram dictionary, which may not be optimal for all tasks or languages. (Methodology section)
critique_points.point_003: The paper does not provide a comprehensive analysis of the computational efficiency of the proposed method compared to existing methods. (Experiments section)
critique_points.point_004: There is limited discussion on the potential limitations or failure cases of the proposed method. (Discussion and Conclusion sections)
critique_points.point_005: The hierarchical CNN architecture used for recombining representations is not thoroughly justified or compared with other possible architectures. (Methodology section)
critique_points.point_006: How does the performance of LaSA compare with other fine-tuning methods that incorporate span-level information? (Understanding the relative effectiveness of the proposed method compared to similar approaches is crucial for evaluating its contribution.)
critique_points.point_007: What are the computational requirements of LaSA compared to existing pre-training methods that incorporate span-level information? (Assessing the computational efficiency is important for understanding the practical applicability of the method.)
critique_points.point_008: How does the choice of the n-gram dictionary affect the performance across different languages and tasks? (The adaptability of the method to various languages and tasks is important for its generalizability.)
